coredata.dc:title: Rapid and sensitive fluorescence determination of oxytocin using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorophores
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yanqu
Liu, Mei
Liu, Xiuxiu
Jiang, Rulan
He, Yunan
Yao, Qian
Chen, Hong
Fu, Chunmei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel nitrogen (N)-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was prepared with quercetin as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source by hydrothermal synthesis, and their application as fluorophores for selective and sensitive determination of oxytocin were reported. The fluorescence quantum yield of the as-prepared N-CDs, which exhibited good water solubility and photostability, was about 6.45 % using rhodamine 6 G as reference substance, and the maximum excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) wavelength were 460 nm and 542 nm, respectively. The results illustrated that the direct fluorescence quenching of N-CDs fluorophore for the detection of oxytocin achieved good linearity in the range of 0.2–5.0 IU/mL and 5.0–10.0 IU/mL, the correlation coefficients were 0.9954 and 0.9909, respectively, and the detection limit was 0.0196 IU/mL (S/N = 3). The recovery rates were 98.8∼103.8 % with RSD= 0.93 %. The interference experiments showed that common metal ions, possible impurities introduced in production and coexisting excipients in the preparation had little adverse influence on selective detection of oxytocin by the developed N-CDs based fluorescent detection method. The mechanism study on the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by oxytocin concentrations under the given experimental conditions demonstrated that there were internal filtration effect and static quenching in the system. The developed fluorescence analysis platform for the detection of oxytocin had been proved to be rapid, sensitive, specific and accurate, and to be used for the quality inspection of oxytocin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence detection
Hydrothermal synthesis
Nitrogen (N)-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
Oxytocin
Fluorescence quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of N-CDs synthesis conditions: (a) Influence of material ratio (The temperature was 210 ℃, the reaction time was 6 h); (b) Influence of reaction temperature (the molar ratio of o-phenylenediamine to quercetin was 3:1, the reaction time was 6 h); (c) Influence of reaction time (the molar ratio of o-phenylenediamine to quercetin was 3:1, the temperature was 220 ℃).

(a) TEM image of N-CDs (Inset: size distribution of N-CDs); (b) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs; (c) UV-Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths; (e) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs.
Investigation of the fluorescence stability of N-CDs (CN-CDs = 0.01 mg/mL). (a) Effect of pH; (b) Effect of NaCl concentrations; (c) Effect of UV irradiation time.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different concentrations of oxytocin (0–10 IU/mL, CN-CDs=1.0 mg/mL)); (b)The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity changes and the concentration of oxytocin at Ex = 460 nm and Em= 542 nm (0.2–5.0 IU/mL, CN-CDs=1.0 mg/mL), (c) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity changes and the concentration of oxytocin at Ex = 460 nm and Em= 542 nm (5.0–10.0 IU/mL, CN-CDs=1.0 mg/mL).
Optimization of detection system conditions. (a) Effect of N-CDs concentration (b) Effect of reaction time; (c) Effect of pH; (d) Effect of NaCl concentration.(Coxytocin = 0.5 IU/mL).
(a) UV spectra of N-CDs, oxytocin, and N-CDs@oxytocin; (b) Potentials of N-CDs, N-CDs@oxytocin;.
Cells viability of at different N-CDs concentrations.
Effects of co-existing substances (C oxytocin=0.01 mg/mL).

row.entry: Concentration in oxytocin in oxytocin injection (IU/mL)

head.ce:title: Rapid and sensitive fluorescence determination of oxytocin using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorophores

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel nitrogen (N)-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was prepared with quercetin as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source by hydrothermal synthesis, and their application as fluorophores for selective and sensitive determination of oxytocin were reported. The fluorescence quantum yield of the as-prepared N-CDs, which exhibited good water solubility and photostability, was about 6.45 % using rhodamine 6 G as reference substance, and the maximum excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) wavelength were 460 nm and 542 nm, respectively. The results illustrated that the direct fluorescence quenching of N-CDs fluorophore for the detection of oxytocin achieved good linearity in the range of 0.2–5.0 IU/mL and 5.0–10.0 IU/mL, the correlation coefficients were 0.9954 and 0.9909, respectively, and the detection limit was 0.0196 IU/mL (S/N = 3). The recovery rates were 98.8∼103.8 % with RSD= 0.93 %. The interference experiments showed that common metal ions, possible impurities introduced in production and coexisting excipients in the preparation had little adverse influence on selective detection of oxytocin by the developed N-CDs based fluorescent detection method. The mechanism study on the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by oxytocin concentrations under the given experimental conditions demonstrated that there were internal filtration effect and static quenching in the system. The developed fluorescence analysis platform for the detection of oxytocin had been proved to be rapid, sensitive, specific and accurate, and to be used for the quality inspection of oxytocin.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytocin is a small molecule polypeptide composed of nine amino acids [1]. Its name derives from its early discovery of a physiological property that induces strong uterine contractions, known as "rapid growth" [2], and it is generally considered a first-line drug for uterine contractions and childbirth process [3,4]. When used in small doses, it can strengthen the contraction of the normal uterus and treat the prolongation of the labor course caused by contractile fatigue. High-dose use of oxytocin causes ankylosing contractions of the uterus to prevent postpartum hemorrhage [5]. Studies have shown that oxytocin produces side effects such as hypotension and coronary vasoconstriction at doses of 5.0–10.0 IU [6]. Therefore, in order to ensure the safety of clinical use of oxytocin, it is necessary to ensure the accuracy of clinical dosage and the content of active pharmaceutical ingredients in the preparation, which also makes the determination of the content of oxytocin injection very important. At present, bioassay and high performance liquid chromatography are used for the determination of oxytocin extracts and preparations. The operation process of biological assays is complex, which requires high operating skills. The animal experiments and anthropometric measurements are easy to introduce large errors [7,8]. HPLC method is simpler and more accurate than bioassay, but requires high cost of sample preparation, equipment and reagents, and long analysis time [9]. HPLC gradient elution method reported in USP and literature takes at least 20 min to analyze a sample and tends to cause baseline drift and reduce reproducibility [10–12]. Fluorescence methods may be superior to other methods due to their merits of rapid response, low cost, and simple operation.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a collection of spherical or quasi-spherical zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles with a size generally less than 10 nm [13]，which are mainly composed of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen elements. Their surface contain a large number of easily modified groups, such as epoxy group, carboxyl group and hydroxyl group, which make the CDs have good water solubility [14,15]. Citric acid, chitosan, glucose, sucrose, etc. are common raw materials for the synthesis of CDs [16]. With the pursuit of green synthesis methods, more and more researchers have used the ingredients of biomass, traditional Chinese medicine and plant as synthetic raw materials to reduce environmental pollution, such as grapefruit peel, tomato [17]. Quercetin is an important component of the flavonoid family with medicinal value, which has good expectorant and antitussive effects, and widely exists in various foods and medicinal plants [18,19]. Quercetin is cheap and easy to extract. It can be obtained in large quantities from nature. Therefore, quercetin can be used as a cheap, non-toxic, environmentally friendly and renewable green raw material for CDs synthesis. The synthesis of CDs can generally be divided into two ways: "top-down" and "bottom-up". The hydrothermal method is one of the commonly used method in the laboratory, which has the characteristics of simple operation, wide carbon source, low cost, and is conducive to large-scale production of CDs [20–24]. CDs are widely used in bio-sensing, disease diagnosis, biological imaging, analysis and detection, drug carriers and other fields [25–29] due to their good luminescence properties and stability, low cytotoxicity, easy preparation, easy access to raw materials, and easy storage [30].

Due to the unique fluorescent properties of CDs, many researchers have applied CDs to the analysis and detection of substances in recent years. When the analyte is added to the CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is enhanced or quenched, and a certain linear relationship can be established to detect the substance. For example, Lingfei Liu et al. used o-phenylenediamine and L-cysteine to prepare nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) by hydrothermal method for sensitive detection of methyl parathion (MP) based on fluorescence quenching mechanism [31]. Xinpei Pang et al. found that ClO- can enhance the fluorescence intensity of N-CD synthesized by nitrogen-rich adenine and ethylenediamine, and realized the effective detection of ClO- [32]. At present, many CDs-based fluorescence analysis methods have been applied to the detection of drugs, metal ions and pesticide residues [33–39].

In this study, low cost and environmentally friendly yellow-green nitrogen (N)-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with Ex = 460 nm and Em = 542 nm were prepared by hydrothermal method using quercetin as carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as nitrogen source. The experimental results showed that N-CDs had good water solubility, optical stability and biocompatibility. We found that there was a decrease in fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with increasing oxytocin concentration. There was a good linear relationship between 0.2 and 5.0 IU/mL and 5.0–10.0 IU/mL with the correlation coefficients were 0.9954 and 0.9909, respectively, and the detection limit was 0.0196 IU/mL (S/N = 3). The recovery rates were 98.8∼103.8 % with RSD= 0.93 %. N-CDs fluorescence was effectively quenched by adding oxytocin into the N-CDs solution based on the static quenching mechanism. The method possessed the merits of convenient, rapid and efficient, and was a kind of analysis method with application prospect.

Quercetin, o-phenylenediamine and Rhodamine 6 G were supplied by Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Oxytocin standard was purchased from the National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products (Beijing, China. Batch number 150529–200902), Oxytocin injection (1 mL: 10 IU) was obtained from Chengdu Haitong Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. Purified water was obtained from ultrapure water machine. All chemicals and reagents were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

The UV absorption spectra were obtained on UV-2300 Ultraviolet-Visible-Spectrophotometer (Shanghai Tianmei Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd.). The fluorescence spectra were carried out in RF-6000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Analytical Instruments Company). The FTIR spectra were measured on IRTracer-100 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Shimadzu Analytical Instruments Corporation, Japan). TEM was conducted by Transmission Electron Microscope (H-600, Hitachi, Japan). Zeta potentials were measured on a NanoZS90 (Malvern Co., Worcestershire, UK) at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity was calculated with 902- ULTS microplate reader (Thermo Fisher). The synthesis of the CDs was performed in PTFE lined high pressure reactor type KH (Shanghai Yushen Instrument Co., Ltd.), CDs were lyophilized using freeze dryer (Wuxi Wo Xinxin Instrument Manufacturing Co., Ltd.).

N-CDs were prepared using a simple bottom-up hydrothermal method. 0.16 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.15 g quercetin（the molar ratio of o-phenylenediamine to quercetin was 3:1）were mixed in 20 mL water with violent stirring. The solution was poured into a 25 mL polyethylene autoclave and reacted at 220 °C for 6 h. The autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature after the reaction. Then, the solution was filtered through 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove impurities, the N-CDs were purified by dialyzing with a dialysis bag (MCWO =1000 Da) against ultrapure water 24 h at room temperature to obtain a purified CDs solution. Finally, the solution was placed in freeze dryer for freeze- drying, and stored at room temperature in the dark for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of N-CDs’ fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs was determined by reference method [40]. Rhodamine 6 G, a fluorescent substance with a fluorescence excitation wavelength and emission wavelength similar to the N-CDs, was selected as the reference substance, and its quantum yield was 95 %. By measuring the absorbance (less than 0.05) and emission spectra of a certain concentration of N-CDs and rhodamine 6 G at the same excitation wavelength, the absorbance and fluorescence integral area were substituted into formula to calculate [41]:
(1)
φ
u
=
φ
s
F
u
A
s
μ
u
2
F
s
A
u
μ
s
2
Where φ is the quantum yield, A stands for absorbance, F represents the integrated area of fluorescence, μ refers to the refractive index of the solvent, s and u correspond to standard and sample, respectively. The refractive indices of water and ethanol are 1.330 and 1.361, respectively.

The oxytocin solution was prepared by weighing a certain amount of oxytocin and adding water to dissolve it, using PBS buffer to adjust the pH value to close to neutral. Then, the solution was transferred into a volumetric flask and the volume was fixed with water. Adding 50 μL of N-CDs (The concentration of N-CDs was 1.0 mg/mL), 50 μL of pure aqueous solution, and 100 μL of different concentrations of oxytocin to a 96-well plate, under the conditions of excitation wavelength 460 nm and emission wavelength 542 nm, fluorescence intensity was determined.

The cytotoxicity of N-CDs was measured by the CCK-8 assay. Firstly, HUVEC cells were collected, the cell suspension concentration was adjusted to 5 × 104 cells/mL, and 100 μL of the cell suspension was added to each well of a 96-well plate and cultured at 37 ℃ in an incubator with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. Secondly, 100 μL of N-CDs at different concentrations were added (each concentration was repeated 6 times) and incubated in a 37 °C incubator for 24 h. Thirdly, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated for 1–4 h. Finally, the absorbance of each well was measured at 450 nm (OD). Cell viability was calculated by the following formula:
(2)
cell viability
%
=
A
( substance )
−
A
( blank )
A
0 substance
−
A
blank
× 100
A (substance): OD value of wells with cells, culture medium, CCK-8 solution and oxytocin;
A (0 substance): OD value of wells with cells, culture medium, CCK-8 solution but no oxytocin;
A (blank): OD value of wells with culture medium, CCK-8 solution but no cells and oxytocin.

In order to construct fluorescent CDs for quantitative analysis of oxytocin, dozens of CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using different carbon and nitrogen sources, including glucose, citric acid, chitosan, ethylene-diamine, ammonium citrate, etc. The study found that the CDs synthesized with quercetin as carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as nitrogen source had a linear relationship of fluorescence quenching with oxytocin. On this basis, the synthesis conditions of N-CDs were optimized by investigating the effect of material ratio, reaction temperature and reaction time on the fluorescence quantum yield of N-CD. The result found that a ratio of 3:1 of o-phenylenediamine to quercetin and heating at 220 °C for 6 h were the best conditions for the synthesis of N-CDs, the fluorescence quantum yield was the highest to 6.45 %.( Fig. 1).

The N-CDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR, UV–vis, etc. The TEM image in Fig. 2(a) demonstrated that the N-CDs were uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of 8.28 nm. Fig. 2(b) showed that the infrared spectrum of N-CDs, the absorption band at 3360 cm−1 and 3031 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of amino and hydroxyl groups, the absorption band at 1621–1460 cm−1 was the stretching vibration peak of the CC double bond on the benzene ring, The absorption band at 1276 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration peak of C-N on the benzene ring, and 745.64 cm−1 was the C-H stretching vibration peak of the ortho-disubstituted benzene ring, indicating that there were functional groups such as hydroxyl and amino groups on the N-CDs surface. The UV–vis absorption spectrum for the N-CDs was illustrated in Fig. 2(c). The N-CDs had two absorption peaks at 280 nm and 230 nm, respectively, which were caused by n→π *, π→π * electronic transition.

The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs was shown in Fig. 2(d) and (e). The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs increased with the increase of the excitation wavelength, and when the excitation wavelength exceeded 460 nm, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly with the increase of excitation wavelength. The maximum excitation wavelength of the N-CDs synthesized in this experiment was 460 nm, and the corresponding maximum emission wavelength was 542 nm.

Fluorescence stability is one of the important properties of CDs and one of the key elements of fluorescence analysis methods. Therefore, the fluorescence stability of N-CDs was investigated in different pH solutions, different NaCl concentration solutions, UV irradiation time and common coexisting systems. Fig. 3(a) showed the effect of PBS solutions with different pH (1.0–10.0) on the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with the same concentration was relatively larger under the near-neutral and weakly alkaline conditions, with the highest in the phosphate solution at pH= 7.0. The N-CDs had almost no fluorescence at pH≤ 4.0. Thus, a PBS buffer solution with pH 7.0 was selected for use in the subsequent experiments. At the same time, fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was stable in high salt solution, indicating that the as-synthesized N-CDs had excellent salt tolerance even when the NaCl concentration reached 70 mg/mL (Fig. 3b). Moreover, we investigated if UV irradiation affected the stability of the N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3(c), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs did not change significantly within eight hours, but decreased slightly after 8 h. It illustrated that UV irradiation had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. At the same time, the results were shown in the table: The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs had no effect on 1000 times of Na+, Fe3+, 100 times of K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni+, Mg2+, 10 times of Cu2+, relative error in ±5 %. ( Table 1).

To evaluate the analytical performance of the N-CDs for oxytocin detection, the fluorescence response of N-CDs was measured by addition of different oxytocin concentrations. As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), a gradual reduction in fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was observed at Ex = 460 nm and Em= 542 nm with oxytocin concentration increasing from 0.2 IU/mL to 10.0 IU/mL. Fig. 4(b) and (c) presented two linear relationships between changes in fluorescence intensity (F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs and N-CDs@oxytocin, respectively) and oxytocin concentration was within the range of 0.2–5.0 IU/mL and 5.0–10.0 IU/ mL. The corresponding regression equations were y = 29486x+17012 (R2 =0.9954) and y = 6536.3x +126483 (R2 =0.9909), respectively (y referred to F0 minus F). The limit of detection of oxytocin was 0.0196 IU/mL（S/N = 3）. The method had the advantages of simple operation process, non-toxicity, high sensitivity and fast response time.

In order to optimize the fluorescence analysis conditions of oxytocin, the effects of N-CDs concentrations, reaction time, pH and salt concentration on fluorescence detection system were investigated. The effects of N-CDs concentrations of 1.3, 1.0, 0.5, 0.25, 0.1, and 0.05 mg/mL on fluorescence determination were shown in Fig. 5(a). When the concentration of N-CDs was 1.0 mg/mL, the linear slope was the largest, suggesting the determination of oxytocin using 1.0 mg/mL N-CDs as fluorophores had higher sensitivity. The effect of reaction time on the N-CDs@oxytocin quenching system was investigated at room temperature. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased immediately after the addition of oxytocin, indicating that the reaction was rapid. Since the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was easily affected by pH, the pH of the detection system needed to be controlled to be neutral. In order to investigate the effect of salt concentration on oxytocin detection, the detection system containing different salt concentrations was studied. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the salt concentration had no effect on the detection system.

It was well known that fluorescence analysis method was easily affected by assay conditions. Therefore, the influences of common metal ions, possible impurities introduced in production, coexisting substances in the preparation, such as inorganic ions, glucose, amino acids, and excipients used in the preparation process of oxytocin, such as trichlorobutanol on the determination were investigated. For the change of fluorescence intensity in the system, if the relative error was with in ±5 %, the influence of the interfering substance on the oxytocin determination was considered to be negligible. As shown in Table 2, the concentrations of these interfering substances were significantly higher than the concentration of oxytocin. All potential interfering substances had a limited effect on the oxytocin assay. All the results were clearly showed that N-CDs-based fluorescence detection was highly selective for oxytocin.

To estimate the practical applicability of the proposed fluorescence method, the as-synthesized N-CDs were used to detect oxytocin in real samples (oxytocin injection) following the standard addition method. In brief, a certain amount of oxytocin standard sample was accurately added to oxytocin injection sample with known oxytocin content to prepare solutions of three concentrations (2.5, 4.0, 8.0 IU/mL), and then analyzed. Recovery tests were performed by comparatively the total amount measured and the total amount added. Relative standard deviation (RSD) was used to describe the precision of the method. The analytical results in Table 3 demonstrated the recovery rates of oxytocin were 98.8∼103.8 % with a RSD of 0.93 % (n = 6). These validation results indicated that the developed fluorescence analysis platform for the detection of oxytocin had good accuracy and precision, the N-CDs had good potential for quantitative analysis of oxytocin in oxytocin injection.

To investigate the quenching mechanism, we used UV–visible absorption, Zeta potential, fluorescence lifetime and Stern Volmer (SV) equation to distinguish static from dynamic quenching. As shown in Fig. 6(a), after the addition of oxytocin, the UV absorption peaks of N-CDs changed slightly, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs might be due to the formation of complexes. With the addition of oxytocin, the potential of the N-CDs changed from −16.2 mv to −6.83 mv, illustrating that the negative potential groups of N-CDs were affected. Fig. 6(c) showed the fluorescence decay curves for N-CDs in the absence or presence of oxytocin. Fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs (τ1) and N-CDs@oxytocin (τ2) were calculated to be 8.9 nsec and 8.67 nsec (τ1/τ2 ≈ 1), respectively, showing a negligible decrease following addition of oxytocin. Therefore, the fluorescence of the obtained N-CDs could be efficiently quenched using oxytoxin because of the effective inner filter effect (IFE) between them. In addition, as shown in Fig. 6(c) and Table 4, the variable temperature experiments found that the fluorescence quenching constant Ksv gradually decreased with the increase of temperature, the quenching rate constant Kq value calculated by Stern Volmer (SV) equation was much larger than 1 × 1010 L•mol−1•s−1, which was in line with the characteristics of static quenching [41,42]. In conclusion, it was speculated that oxytocin reduced the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs through IFE and static quenching.
(c) Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs (τ1) and the N-CDs@oxytocin system (τ2); (d) Stern-Volmer plots of N-CDs-oxytocin system at different temperatures.

The cytotoxicity of different concentrations of N-CDs on HUVEC cells was determined by CCK-8. As shown in Fig. 7, when the concentration of N-CDs was as high as 600 μg/mL, the cell viability was still higher than 80 %, indicating that N-CDs had good biocompatibility and low toxicity.

In summary, N-CDs with yellow-green fluorescence were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using quercetin and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials and characterized by different techniques. The N-CDs showed good water solubility, high dispersibility, and optical properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 6.45 %. The low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of the N-CDs were indicated by CCK-8 assay. The fluorescence of the obtained N-CDs could be selectively and efficiently quenched by oxytocin with a detection limit of 0.0196 IU/mL. The N-CDs were used for oxytocin detection in oxytocin injection with a recovery of 98.8∼103.8 %. The quenching mechanism of the N-CDs@oxytocin system was static quenching. It is noteworthy that because the N-CDs were easily affected by pH, and the fluorescence is unstable at different pH values, attention should be paid to the adjustment of pH value in the detection process. To our knowledge, this was the first report of a CDs-fluorophore developed for the determination of oxytocin. In addition, this study set a good example for rapid and sensitive determination of drugs.

Yanqu Zhou: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Methodology. Mei Liu: Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xiuxiu Liu: Methodology, Validation. Rulan Jiang: Investigation, Methodology. Yunan He: Investigation, Methodology. Qian Yao: Validation, Resources. Hong Chen: Investigation, Resources. Chunmei Fu: Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Engineering Research Center for Pharmaceuticals and Equipments of Sichuan Province Fund (No. ERCPEF2202), Key Laboratory of Chemical Drug Quality Research and control of Sichuan Drug Administration Fund (No. 2022HZKF-01), Sichuan University --Dazhou Municipal People's Government Strategic Cooperation Fund (No. 2021CDDZ-20), Sichuan Province Natural Science Foundation (No. 2019YFS0092).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current and future techniques for detecting oxytocin: focusing on genetically-encoded GPCR sensors
J. Neurosci. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lee D., Kwon H.-B. Current and future techniques for detecting oxytocin: Focusing on genetically-encoded GPCR sensors [J]. Journal of Neuroscience Methods, 2022, 366: 109407.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxytocin and the microbiome
Curr. Opin. Endocr. Metab. Res.
